meta.title: Cloud Pavilion — Considered journeys through Jiangxi
meta.description: Bespoke journeys through China's most considered landscape. Mist mountains, ancient kilns, and a thousand years of slow.

footer.newsletter: We don't run a newsletter. Speak to a designer instead.

hero.heading: Jiangxi, considered.
hero.sub: Porcelain turned by hand. Temples older than language. A province most maps forget — and the journeys we draw, in private, to reach it.

bridge.heading: From Fez to Jingdezhen — a thousand years of considered craft.
bridge.body: Travel here is not a list. It is the rhythm of porcelain turned by hand, the climb to a temple older than language, tea served on a balcony above the clouds. We trace lineages that quietly resemble your own — the inheritance of craft, the geography of contemplation, the long arc of trade.

journeys.heading: Five days. Eight days. Twelve.
journeys.sub: Each itinerary is a starting point — a way of thinking, not a product. Every detail yours to shape.

destinations.heading: Five regions. One inheritance.
destinations.sub: Each region is a story. We weave them in the order that best fits your hours.

items.title: Craft & Ceramics
items.body: A thousand-year unbroken lineage at the porcelain capital. The studios that open only by introduction — and only to us.

items.title: Mountain Pilgrimage
items.body: Five sacred peaks. Monks before dawn, mist by mid-morning, and the cold quiet of stone older than dynasty.

items.title: Tea & Tradition
items.body: Lushan Yunwu, picked at the cloud line in May. Ceremonies in pavilions that have poured for emperors and farmers without distinction.

items.title: Landscape & Photography
items.body: Rapeseed gold in late March. The cloud sea in October. We know the hour the light arrives, and the hour to leave.

atelierTeaser.heading: Designed in private. Travelled in confidence.
atelierTeaser.body: Cloud Pavilion is a small practice. Our designers spend more time listening than briefing — your journey arrives with the considered weight of months of preparation, and the lightness of a thing made well.

journalTeaser.heading: Field notes from a quiet province.

inquireCta.heading: Every journey begins with a conversation.
inquireCta.sub: One working day to a reply. WhatsApp, email, or the quiet inquiry form — whichever suits the hour.

hero.heading: Three movements through Jiangxi.
hero.sub: Five days, eight days, twelve days. Each a starting point — every detail yours to shape.

index.intro: Below are our three flagship itineraries. Each can be lengthened, layered, or reshaped. They are templates of thinking, not products. Your final journey will be drawn around your own questions.

items.title: Essential Jiangxi
items.tagline: The essence — mist mountains and ancient villages.
items.summary: A first introduction to the province. Nanchang's literary pavilion, Wuyuan's rapeseed valleys, Jingdezhen's porcelain workshops, Lushan's tea-mist mornings. Paced for those who want the heart of Jiangxi without sacrificing its quiet.
items.intro: Five days is a careful number. Long enough to step into the rhythm of Jiangxi, short enough to leave wanting another week. We have built Essential as a measured arc — pavilion at the centre of dynasty, then west to the village valleys, north to the porcelain city, and finally up into the cloud-quiet of Lushan.
highlights: Sunset at Tengwang Pavilion — where a Tang poet rewrote Chinese literature
Two nights in a 600-year-old Hui village courtyard
Private ceramic workshop with a Jingdezhen master
Lushan Yunwu tea ceremony, served on the mountain

days.title: Arrive Nanchang
days.body: Met at Changbei airport. Settle into a quiet hotel above the Gan river. Late afternoon at Tengwang Pavilion, the literary tower rebuilt twenty-eight times across thirteen centuries. Dinner of Jiangxi specialties, hosted by your designer.

days.title: Nanchang to Wuyuan
days.body: Slow drive north-east through tea hills. Arrival in Likeng village by lunch. The afternoon is unscheduled — wander between stone bridges, white-walled lanes, ancestral halls. Dinner at a village table.

days.title: Wuyuan to Jingdezhen
days.body: Morning in Huangling, the hanging-field village. Lunch at the foot of the kilns. Afternoon in a working studio with a sixth-generation porcelain master — wheel, glaze, mark. You will leave with a piece you shaped yourself.

days.title: Jingdezhen to Lushan
days.body: A long lunch at Taoxichuan Art Avenue, then the climb to Lushan. Donglin Temple at dusk — the courtyard lanterns lit one by one. The mountain air thins; the valley falls away. A pot of Yunwu in the room before sleep.

days.title: Lushan to Nanchang, departure
days.body: Dawn tea ceremony on the mountain. Slow descent through cloud. Lunch in Nanchang. Onward flight.

items.title: Jiangxi in Depth
items.tagline: The signature — temples, mist, and Taoist sacred peaks.
items.summary: Eight days across four UNESCO landscapes. The Taoist heart of China — Longhu's red cliffs, Sanqingshan's granite gods — woven into the literary, ceramic and Buddhist threads of the Essential.
items.intro: Eight days lets us trace four threads — literary, ceramic, Buddhist, Taoist — without rushing any one of them. The Taoist heart of Jiangxi reveals itself slowly: bamboo on a red-cliff river, a priest's morning chant, the stone goddess watching from her peak.
highlights: Four UNESCO landscapes, traced in a single arc
Bamboo raft beneath Longhu's hanging coffins — placed in the cliffs two thousand years ago by means no scholar has explained
A night above the kilns in a working ceramic studio-hotel
Donglin Temple's morning chant — the bell sounds three times before five
Oriental Goddess Peak at sunrise, before the cable cars open

days.title: Arrive Nanchang
days.body: Tengwang Pavilion at golden hour. Dinner above the river.

days.title: Nanchang to Wuyuan
days.body: Likeng by lunch. Afternoon walks through Hui-village lanes.

days.title: Wuyuan to Jingdezhen
days.body: Huangling at dawn. Private studio session by afternoon. Studio-hotel overnight.

days.title: Jingdezhen to Longhu
days.body: Bamboo raft past the cliff burials of the Guyue. Tianshi Mansion, where the Celestial Masters have presided since the second century. A quiet hour with a resident priest.

days.title: Longhu to Lushan
days.body: Drive north as the red cliffs give way to tea hills. Donglin Temple by dusk; lanterns in the courtyard. Yunwu poured on the mountain.

days.title: Lushan to Sanqingshan
days.body: Cable car to the Five Elements complex. Cliff-face boardwalk above a sea of cloud. A mountain lodge — no signal, woodsmoke at dusk.

days.title: Sanqingshan dawn, return Nanchang
days.body: Oriental Goddess Peak at sunrise — the stone pillar that has watched the valley longer than recorded history. Slow descent. Afternoon at Poyang Lake or with a Nanchang silk-weaver.

days.title: Departure
days.body: Onward flight.

items.title: Heritage & Mist
items.tagline: The pilgrimage — 1,500 years of sacred Jiangxi.
items.summary: Twelve days. Five UNESCO sites. Buddhist monks, Taoist priests, porcelain masters, tea farmers — and the unhurried hours in between. A journey for those who travel to listen.
items.intro: Twelve days is the journey we would design for ourselves. Five UNESCO sites, two multi-night retreats, a Wugong dawn above the cloud sea, and a capstone evening at a cliff-edge hotel suspended in a sacred gorge. Every hour considered. None of them rushed.
highlights: Five UNESCO landscapes — Lushan, Longhu, Sanqingshan, Jingdezhen, Wuyuan
Private mentorship at a sixth-generation porcelain studio
Two nights inside Donglin Temple — dawn chant, vegetarian table, monk-led walks
Wugong Mountain pre-dawn — when the first peaks break the cloud sea
A final evening at Wangxian Valley, in a suite carved into the cliff face
Optional hours with a calligrapher, a tea master, or a Buddhist scholar

days.title: Arrive Nanchang
days.body: Tengwang Pavilion, the City of Porcelain exhibition, the night market by the river.

days.title: Nanchang
days.body: Quiet morning. Afternoon with a calligraphy master. Dinner with a Jiangxi historian.

days.title: Wuyuan
days.body: Two nights in Likeng. Rapeseed fields, drying rooftops, Hui carving workshop.

days.title: Jingdezhen
days.body: Full-day private studio mentorship. Studio-hotel overnight.

days.title: Longhu Mountain
days.body: Bamboo raft, Tianshi Mansion, a Taoist priest's teaching with translator.

days.title: Lushan retreat
days.body: Two nights at Donglin Temple. Dawn ceremonies. Multi-peak hiking. White Deer Cave Academy. Tea ceremony at the cloud line.

days.title: Sanqingshan
days.body: Five Elements temple complex at first light. Afternoon with a stone-carver whose father carved the same mountain.

days.title: Wugong Mountain
days.body: Pre-dawn ascent. The peaks rise from the cloud sea one by one. Afternoon in the bamboo, walking slowly and saying little.

days.title: Wangxian Valley
days.body: A suite cut into the cliff face. Dinner served on a terrace that hangs over the gorge — the lights of the valley a thousand feet below.

days.title: Return Nanchang, departure
days.body: Onward flight.

hero.heading: Five regions. One inheritance.
hero.sub: Each region is a story. We weave them in the order that best fits your hours.

items.title: Wuyuan
items.subtitle: Rapeseed valleys, framed by 500-year villages.
items.hook: Every March and April, six thousand hectares of gold unfurl beneath whitewashed Hui rooftops. The valley has not changed in five centuries.
items.intro: Wuyuan is a county of villages, not a town. To arrive here is to leave the highway grid and enter a slower geometry — rivers cross paths, stone bridges arc, white walls reflect the colour of the season. In spring it is gold; in autumn, the rooftops turn red with drying chillies and corn. The Hui-style architecture, lifted from sixteenth-century Anhui merchant fortunes, has been preserved here more completely than anywhere else in China.
items.best: Late March to early April for rapeseed; October to November for autumn rooftop colour
experiences: Walk Likeng's stone bridges before breakfast, when the village is still its own
Dinner inside an ancestral hall, set by a village elder
An afternoon at the bench with a sixth-generation Hui woodcarver
Calligraphy at dawn, on a table set by the river

items.title: Lushan
items.subtitle: Two hundred temples on a single mountain.
items.hook: UNESCO World Heritage since 1996. Buddhist pilgrims have climbed this mountain for 1,300 years; emperors and poets followed. The mist arrives like clockwork.
items.intro: Lushan is not a mountain so much as a library. Every peak holds a temple, every temple a manuscript, every manuscript a thousand years of consideration. The Song-dynasty landscape painters lived here. The neo-Confucian school of White Deer Cave Academy was founded here. The first Pure Land Buddhist monastery in China was built here in 386. Even today, the morning chant of Donglin Temple begins at five, and the bell carries down the valley.
items.best: May for tea harvest; September to November for clear mornings and cloud seas
experiences: Yunwu poured in a Tang-era hermit's pavilion, at the hour the mist arrives
Donglin's morning chant — the hall is colder than the night air; sit at the back
An hour at White Deer Cave Academy, where Zhu Xi taught the neo-Confucian school
The Three Peaks ridge at last light, when the valley empties of voices

items.title: Jingdezhen
items.subtitle: One thousand years of porcelain.
items.hook: Since 1004 CE, every piece fired in this city bore the reign mark of its emperor. The kilns still burn. The masters still teach.
items.intro: Jingdezhen is the porcelain capital of the world by a margin so wide that no rival even competes for the title. For a thousand unbroken years, the city has fired clay. Its blue-and-white travelled the Maritime Silk Road; its imperial wares set the standard for global porcelain. Today the masters live in Sanbao, work in Taoxichuan, teach in private studios that open only by introduction. Cloud Pavilion is one of those introductions.
items.best: October to April for cooler studio days; spring for international ceramic fairs
experiences: A session at the wheel with a contemporary master — clay, glaze, mark
The Ancient Kiln complex, where dragon kilns still fire on the old schedule
Taoxichuan at twilight, when the studios open and the alleys fill
A piece you shaped yourself, fired and shipped to your door

items.title: Longhu Mountain
items.subtitle: The birthplace of Taoism, on red cliffs.
items.hook: 1,900 years ago, Zhang Daoling founded the Celestial Masters school on this red-rock landscape. The hanging coffins of an older people still cling to the cliffs above the river.
items.intro: Longhu — Dragon-Tiger Mountain — is one of Taoism's four sacred peaks and, by most accounts, its first. The Tianshi Mansion at its foot has housed the Celestial Masters since the second century. The Luxi river that winds between its red Danxia cliffs carries bamboo rafts past a stranger geography still: the hanging coffins of the ancient Guyue people, placed in cliff caves two thousand years ago by means no scholar has yet fully explained.
items.best: April to June and September to November for mild weather and the clearest river days
experiences: Bamboo raft down the Luxi, past the hanging coffins of the Guyue
Tianshi Mansion — eighteen centuries of unbroken Celestial Master lineage
A quiet hour with a resident priest, translated word for word
The Sky Walk at sunrise, before the cliff face warms to red

items.title: Sanqingshan
items.subtitle: Forty-eight granite peaks. A Taoist cathedral.
items.hook: UNESCO World Heritage. One billion years of erosion, sculpted into pillars and dragons. The Oriental Goddess has watched the valley for longer than recorded history.
items.intro: Sanqingshan is named for the Three Pure Ones of Taoist cosmology, and the mountain itself appears to have been carved with the theology in mind. Forty-eight granite peaks rise above a sea of cloud. The Oriental Goddess, a 128-metre stone pillar, sits in the lotus position at the summit. The Taoist temple complex — 238 documented cultural sites — was laid out 1,600 years ago. Walking the cliff-face boardwalks at dawn, before the cable cars open, is one of the quieter experiences in modern China.
items.best: April to June for wildflowers; September to November for cloud seas
experiences: Sunrise at the Oriental Goddess — a 128-metre pillar in lotus position above the cloud
The Five Elements complex, walked between temples laid out 1,600 years ago
Cliff-face boardwalks before the cable cars open, with a private guide
A mountain lodge with no signal, and stars the cities have forgotten

hero.heading: Designed in private. Travelled in confidence.
hero.sub: Cloud Pavilion is a small practice. We design fewer journeys, and we design them better.

philosophy.heading: Travel is a sequence of decisions about attention.
philosophy.body1: What to see. What to skip. Where to stay a third night. Where to leave at noon. We are paid to make those decisions well, on your behalf — and to disappear once they are made.
philosophy.body2: We do not run tours. We do not keep a list of departures. Every journey is drawn from one conversation and shaped over weeks. By the time you land in Nanchang, every door is open, every host expecting you, every hour considered.

partnership.heading: Our standing in Jiangxi.
partnership.body: Cloud Pavilion travels through Jiangxi by way of an institutional partnership with one of the province's leading universities — a relationship that gives our guests facilitated visa processing, access to private cultural venues, and the trust of local communities not granted to ordinary tour operators.

team.heading: A small group of people who answer the phone.
team.body: We are designers, fixers, drivers, monks, ceramicists, and tea farmers — distributed between Casablanca and Nanchang. We do not have a customer service department. The person who designs your journey is the same person who picks up when you call from the road.

items.title: Fewer journeys, better journeys.
items.body: We cap the calendar. When the calendar is full, we say so.

items.title: We do not write itineraries we have not walked.
items.body: Every itinerary on this site has been travelled by a Cloud Pavilion designer in the past eighteen months.

items.title: We never auto-confirm.
items.body: Every inquiry is read by a human, replied to by a human, and quoted by a human.

items.title: We do not pad.
items.body: If a journey would be better in seven days than in eight, we will recommend seven. We do not bill by the night.

items.title: Discretion is a service.
items.body: We do not publish client names. We do not photograph guests. What you tell us stays with us.

cta.heading: Begin a conversation.
cta.body: Tell us what draws you. We will write back within a working day.

hero.heading: Field notes from a quiet province.
hero.sub: Essays on craft, mountain, and trade, written from the road.

items.title: The Weight of a Thousand Years of Clay
items.subtitle: Notes from a Jingdezhen studio.
items.lede: There is no romance in clay until you have spent an hour failing to centre it. The master watches without speaking. You begin again.
body: The studio sits at the end of a stone alley in Sanbao. Its courtyard is a kiln older than the United States. The master — a sixth-generation potter whose family mark has appeared on imperial commissions since the Ming — pours tea in silence while I wrestle the wheel.

What strikes me, eventually, is the patience of the place. Jingdezhen has been making porcelain for one thousand years. A thousand years is enough time for an entire civilisation to forget how impatient our own century is.
When the piece finally rises, it is mine — flawed, lopsided, undeniably mine. The master nods. He does not need to say anything. The clay has already taught what it teaches.
We travel here because the lesson is not in the tea house or the kiln gallery. It is in the hour at the wheel.

items.title: What the Mountain Knows
items.subtitle: A dawn at Donglin Temple.
items.lede: Donglin Temple was founded in 386. It has watched twenty-eight dynasties rise and fall. At five in the morning, it watches you arrive.
body: We climbed Lushan the night before, in the dark. The taxi could only take us so far. From there, the cobbled path. From there, the stone steps. From there, the mist.
Donglin's morning chant begins at five. The hall is colder than the night air. The monks — there are eleven of them — assemble without speaking. The bell sounds three times. The chant begins.
For an hour we sit at the back. The chant is in classical Chinese. We understand nothing. We understand everything.
Outside, the mist lifts off the valley. The first pilgrim of the day arrives. Lushan has been receiving visitors like this for one thousand three hundred years.

items.title: The First Week of Gold
items.subtitle: Wuyuan, late March.
items.lede: For one week each year, the valleys of Wuyuan turn gold. The villagers know to within three days when it will begin.
body: Mr Wang has been a farmer in Likeng for forty-seven years. His father was a farmer here. His grandfather was a farmer here. He knows the valley the way an old reader knows a poem.

We arrive on a Tuesday. By Wednesday morning, the first valley is half gold. By Friday, every south-facing slope is a sheet of luminous yellow. The white walls of the village reflect the colour like a paper lantern reflects a flame.

Photography is everywhere. Bus tours come, take pictures, leave. Mr Wang serves us tea and watches the visitors with mild interest. 'They take a picture,' he says, 'and they leave. They never see the second week.'
The second week of gold is when the petals begin to fall, and the bees arrive in earnest, and the whole valley smells like honey for ten days. That is the week we tell our guests to come.

hero.heading: Begin the conversation.
hero.sub: Five short questions. A working-day reply. No newsletter.

contact.label: How shall we reach you?

fields.name: Your name
fields.notes: Anything we should know before we write back?

contact.consent: We will only use your details to reply to this inquiry.

confirmation.heading: We have it.
confirmation.body: Your inquiry is now on a designer's desk. We will write back within one working day — in your language. Visa support is included with every journey.

common.whatsappPrefill: Hello Cloud Pavilion — I would like to ask about a journey to Jiangxi.

notFound.heading: This pavilion is empty.
notFound.body: The page you were looking for has wandered into the mist. Begin again from the home page, or write to us.
